questions.question: What is the risk of ovarian cancer at age 70 in BRCA1 carriers?
questions.explanation: BRCA1 mutation carriers have a cumulative risk of ovarian cancer of approximately 39-46% at age 70, while that of BRCA2 is lower (11-17%).

questions.question: What histological type of ovarian cancer is most associated with BRCA mutations?
questions.explanation: High-grade serous carcinoma is the histological subtype most frequently associated with BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations, representing approximately 70% of hereditary cases.

questions.question: What is the DNA repair mechanism affected by BRCA mutations?
questions.explanation: The BRCA1 and BRCA2 proteins participate in the repair of DNA double-strand breaks through homologous recombination, and their dysfunction leads to genomic instability.

questions.question: What drug takes advantage of BRCA deficiency to induce cell death?
questions.explanation: Olaparib is a PARP inhibitor that exploits the homologous recombination repair deficiency in BRCA-mutated tumors, causing synthetic lethality.

questions.question: What is the inheritance of BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations associated with ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Mutations in BRCA1 and BRCA2 are inherited in an autosomal dominant manner with incomplete penetrance, so the risk is transmitted to offspring with a 50% probability.

questions.question: What procedure reduces the risk of ovarian cancer in BRCA carriers?
questions.explanation: Risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy reduces the risk of ovarian and fallopian cancer by up to 80% in BRCA mutation carriers, and is recommended between 35-40 years of age.

questions.question: What is the most common symptom of ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Abdominal distention is the most common symptom in ovarian cancer, due to the presence of ascites or tumor mass that occupies space in the abdominal cavity.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism explains abdominal distension in ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Abdominal distension in ovarian cancer is mainly due to ascites, caused by peritoneal dissemination of tumor cells that alter vascular permeability and fluid reabsorption.

questions.question: What is the first imaging study in suspected ovarian cancer with abdominal distension?
questions.explanation: Transvaginal ultrasound is the initial study recommended to evaluate adnexal masses and ascites, allowing the ovarian lesion to be characterized and to guide the diagnostic suspicion.

questions.question: What tumor marker is most frequently elevated in epithelial ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: CA 125 is the most used tumor marker in epithelial ovarian cancer, being elevated in approximately 80% of advanced cases, although it is not specific.

questions.question: What is the main route of spread of ovarian cancer that causes ascites?
questions.explanation: Peritoneal seeding is the main route of spread of ovarian cancer, since tumor cells detach and implant in the peritoneum, causing ascites and abdominal distension.

questions.question: What surgical treatment is of choice in advanced ovarian cancer with ascites?
questions.explanation: Optimal cytoreduction (surgery that leaves residual tumor <1 cm) is the standard surgical treatment in advanced ovarian cancer, improving survival by reducing tumor burden and ascites.

questions.question: What is the most used tumor marker in the monitoring of ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: CA-125 is the serum marker most used in the monitoring of epithelial ovarian cancer, although it is not specific and can be elevated in other benign and malignant conditions.

questions.question: What type of ovarian cancer is most frequently associated with elevated CA-125?
questions.explanation: Epithelial ovarian carcinomas, especially high-grade serous, are those that most frequently elevate CA-125, unlike germ cell or stromal tumors.

questions.question: In what stage of ovarian cancer does CA-125 have the greatest diagnostic utility?
questions.explanation: CA-125 has greater sensitivity in advanced stages (III and IV) of ovarian cancer, while in early stages its elevation is less frequent and specific.

questions.question: Which of the following benign conditions can falsely elevate CA-125?
questions.explanation: Endometriosis is a common benign cause of elevated CA-125, as are pelvic inflammatory disease, fibroids, and pregnancy, which limits its specificity.

questions.question: What approximate percentage of women with epithelial ovarian cancer have elevated CA-125 at diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Approximately 80-90% of patients with epithelial ovarian cancer have elevated CA-125 at the time of diagnosis, although in early stages the sensitivity is lower.

questions.question: What is the main limitation of CA-125 as a screening test for ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: The low specificity of CA-125 limits its use as population screening, since it can be elevated in multiple benign gynecological and non-gynecological processes, generating false positives.

questions.question: What is the typical ultrasound finding of early-stage ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: The complex solid-cystic mass with thick septa or vegetations is the most characteristic ultrasound finding of ovarian cancer, especially in early stages, unlike simple benign cysts.

questions.question: Which component in an adnexal mass suggests malignancy on ultrasound?
questions.explanation: Thick septa (greater than 3 mm) and mural nodules or vegetations are ultrasound signs of high risk of malignancy in adnexal masses, according to the IOTA criteria.

questions.question: What tumor marker is most frequently associated with epithelial ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: CA 125 is the serum biomarker most used in the diagnosis and monitoring of epithelial ovarian cancer, although it is not specific and can be elevated in benign processes.

questions.question: What is the surgical treatment of choice for advanced ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Optimal cytoreduction (surgery that leaves residual implants less than 1 cm) is the surgical standard in advanced ovarian cancer, associated with a better prognosis.

questions.question: What histological type is the most common in ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: High-grade serous carcinoma represents approximately 70% of epithelial ovarian cancers, being the most common and aggressive subtype.

questions.question: What is the first line of systemic treatment in advanced ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: The combination of paclitaxel and carboplatin constitutes the first-line chemotherapy regimen in advanced ovarian cancer, based on clinical trials that demonstrate better survival.

questions.question: What is the standard surgical approach for staging ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Exploratory laparotomy is the mandatory standard surgical approach for staging ovarian cancer, as it allows complete exploration of the abdominal cavity and performance of staging procedures.

questions.question: What procedure is part of the surgical staging of ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenectomy is an essential component of surgical staging of ovarian cancer, as it evaluates lymphatic spread of the disease.

questions.question: What is the main objective of laparotomy in ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: The main goal of staging laparotomy in ovarian cancer is optimal cytoreduction, which consists of resecting as much tumor as possible to improve prognosis.

questions.question: What surgical sample is mandatory in the staging of ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Peritoneal cytology (peritoneal lavage) is a mandatory surgical sample in the staging of ovarian cancer, as it detects malignant cells in the abdominal cavity.

questions.question: What structure is systematically evaluated during staging laparotomy?
questions.explanation: The cul-de-sac of Douglas is systematically evaluated during staging laparotomy for ovarian cancer, as it is a common site of peritoneal dissemination.

questions.question: Which finding contraindicates immediate complete surgical staging?
questions.explanation: Extensive metastatic disease may contraindicate immediate full surgical staging, as optimal cytoreduction is not feasible and neoadjuvant chemotherapy is preferred.

questions.question: What is the characteristic tumor marker for endodermal sinus type ovarian germ cell tumor?
questions.explanation: Endodermal sinus type germ cell tumor produces alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) as a characteristic marker, while beta-hCG is elevated in choriocarcinoma and CA-125 is more typical of epithelial tumors.

questions.question: Which ovarian germ cell tumor is classically associated with elevated beta-hCG?
questions.explanation: Ovarian choriocarcinoma, derived from germ cells, produces beta-hCG due to its syncytiotrophoblastic differentiation, unlike endodermal sinus which secretes AFP and dysgerminoma which can elevate LDH.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for an ovarian germ cell tumor with elevated AFP and beta-hCG?
questions.explanation: The BEP regimen (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) is the standard first-line chemotherapy treatment for ovarian germ cell tumors, as they are highly chemosensitive and seek to preserve fertility.

questions.question: Which imaging finding is most suggestive of ovarian germ cell tumor?
questions.explanation: Germ cell tumors, especially immature teratoma, usually present a heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic components, and may contain calcifications (fat, calcium), unlike simple benign cysts.

questions.question: What is the typical age of presentation for ovarian germ cell tumors?
questions.explanation: Ovarian germ cell tumors are more common in adolescents and young women (under 30 years of age), unlike epithelial tumors that predominate in perimenopause and postmenopause.

questions.question: What additional marker is usually elevated in dysgerminoma, another germ cell tumor?
questions.explanation: Dysgerminoma, the most common germ cell tumor, frequently elevates lactic dehydrogenase (LDH) as a serum marker, while AFP and beta-hCG are elevated in endodermal sinus and choriocarcinoma, respectively.

questions.question: What is the characteristic hormonal marker of granulosa cell tumor?
questions.explanation: Granulosa cell tumor produces inhibin B, which is a specific and useful serum marker for diagnosis and monitoring, unlike CA-125 or HE4, which are less specific.

questions.question: What genetic alteration is most frequently associated with adult granulosa cell tumor?
questions.explanation: The FOXL2 mutation (C134W) is characteristic of adult-type granulosa cell tumor, present in more than 90% of cases, which helps in the differential diagnosis.

questions.question: What is the standard surgical treatment for early-stage granulosa cell tumor?
questions.explanation: The standard surgical treatment is bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with hysterectomy, due to its indolent behavior but with the risk of late recurrence; Conservative surgery is reserved in selected cases.

questions.question: What histologic feature is typical of granulosa cell tumor?
questions.explanation: Call-Exner bodies are microscopic structures characteristic of granulosa cell tumor, consisting of small cavities filled with eosinophilic material surrounded by tumor cells.

questions.question: What is the most common endocrine complication in patients with granulosa cell tumor?
questions.explanation: The granulosa cell tumor secretes estrogen, causing hyperestrogenism, which manifests clinically as abnormal uterine bleeding in premenopausal women or precocious pseudopuberty in girls.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic recurrence pattern of granulosa cell tumor?
questions.explanation: Granulosa cell tumor has a characteristic late recurrence, often after 5 or more years, requiring prolonged follow-up with inhibin B measurement and imaging.

questions.question: In ovarian cancer, optimal cytoreductive surgery is defined by a maximum residual tumor of:
questions.explanation: Optimal cytoreductive surgery is defined as a residual tumor less than 1 cm in its maximum diameter, as this improves survival and response to adjuvant chemotherapy.

questions.question: What is the standard oncological approach in the surgical treatment of advanced ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Standard surgical treatment includes total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, and omentectomy, as well as cytoreduction of peritoneal implants.

questions.question: What anatomical structure is necessarily removed in bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy?
questions.explanation: Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy involves the surgical removal of both fallopian tubes and both ovaries, preserving the uterus if hysterectomy is not indicated.

questions.question: What is the main objective of hysterectomy in ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: The primary goal of hysterectomy in ovarian cancer is to achieve optimal cytoreduction, removing all visible tumor to improve overall survival.

questions.question: What additional procedure is performed along with hysterectomy and salpingo-oophorectomy in advanced stages?
questions.explanation: In advanced stages of ovarian cancer, omentectomy is performed, since the omentum is a frequent site for tumor implants and its resection improves oncological control.

questions.question: What is the preferred approach for cytoreductive surgery in ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Infraumbilical midline laparotomy is the route of choice because it allows adequate abdominal exploration, complete cytoreduction, and management of peritoneal implants.

questions.question: What is the mechanism of action of paclitaxel in adjuvant chemotherapy for ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Paclitaxel is a taxane that stabilizes microtubules, preventing their depolymerization and stopping cell mitosis in the G2/M phase, which induces apoptosis in cancer cells.

questions.question: What is the main dose-limiting adverse effect of carboplatin in the adjuvant regimen for ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia and neutropenia, is the dose-limiting adverse effect of carboplatin, unlike cisplatin whose main toxicity is renal.

questions.question: At what stage of ovarian cancer is adjuvant chemotherapy with carboplatin and paclitaxel indicated after cytoreductive surgery?
questions.explanation: Adjuvant chemotherapy with carboplatin and paclitaxel is indicated in stages IC or higher, as well as in stages IA/IB with high-risk histology, after optimal cytoreductive surgery.

questions.question: What is the standard dose of carboplatin in the adjuvant regimen for ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: The carboplatin dose is calculated based on the area under the curve (AUC) using the Calvert formula, with AUC 5-6 every 3 weeks being the standard dose in combination with adjuvant paclitaxel.

questions.question: What is the main neurological toxicity associated with paclitaxel in the adjuvant treatment of ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Peripheral sensory neuropathy, manifesting as paresthesias and numbness in the hands and feet, is the most common and cumulative neurological toxicity of paclitaxel.

questions.question: What administration schedule is standard for paclitaxel in combination with carboplatin in adjuvant ovarian cancer?
options: Paclitaxel 175 mg/m² in 3 hours + carboplatin AUC 5-6
questions.explanation: The standard regimen is paclitaxel 175 mg/m² as a 3-hour infusion followed by carboplatin AUC 5-6, administered every 3 weeks for 6 cycles, after optimal cytoreductive surgery.

questions.question: What is the most used tumor marker in the monitoring of high-grade serous ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: CA 125 is the tumor marker of choice for monitoring epithelial ovarian cancer, especially high-grade serous cancer, although it is not specific.

questions.question: Which genetic mutation is most frequently associated with high-grade serous ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: The mutation in TP53 is present in more than 95% of cases of high-grade serous ovarian cancer, being a distinctive characteristic of this subtype.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for advanced high-grade serous ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Standard treatment for advanced high-grade serous ovarian cancer includes cytoreductive surgery followed by platinum-taxane chemotherapy, or neoadjuvant chemotherapy in selected cases.

questions.question: What is the most common route of spread of high-grade serous ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: The spread of high-grade serous ovarian cancer occurs mainly by peritoneal seeding, with implants in the peritoneum and omentum, which explains its frequent advanced presentation.

questions.question: Which histologic finding is characteristic of high-grade serous ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: High-grade serous ovarian cancer is characterized by complex papillae, severe nuclear atypia, frequent mitoses, and necrosis, unlike low-grade tumors.

questions.question: What is the most common location of origin for high-grade serous ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation: Recent studies indicate that high-grade serous ovarian cancer frequently originates in the fimbriae of the fallopian tube, not directly in the ovary.

questions.question: What is the classic triad of Meigs syndrome?
questions.explanation: Meigs syndrome is characterized by the presence of ascites, pleural effusion, and a benign ovarian tumor (usually fibroma), which resolve after removal of the tumor.

questions.question: What type of ovarian tumor is typically associated with Meigs syndrome?
questions.explanation: Ovarian fibroma is the benign tumor most frequently related to Meigs syndrome, although granulosa tumors or thecomas may also be associated.

questions.question: What is the proposed pathophysiological mechanism of pleural effusion in Meigs syndrome?
options: Passage of ascitic fluid through diaphragmatic defects
questions.explanation: It is believed that the ascitic fluid passes into the pleural cavity through defects or lymphatics of the diaphragm, generating pleural effusion that disappears when the tumor is removed.

questions.question: What characteristic of ascitic fluid is usually observed in Meigs syndrome?
questions.explanation: The ascitic fluid in Meigs syndrome is usually a transudate or serohemorrhagic exudate, without malignant cells, which helps differentiate it from peritoneal carcinomatosis.

questions.question: What is the definitive treatment for Meigs syndrome?
questions.explanation: Surgical resection of the ovarian tumor (oophorectomy or adnexectomy) is the definitive treatment, since ascites and pleural effusion disappear spontaneously after removal.

questions.question: What cytological finding in the pleural or ascitic fluid supports the diagnosis of Meigs syndrome?
questions.explanation: In Meigs syndrome, cytology of the pleural and ascitic fluid is negative for malignant cells, which distinguishes it from peritoneal carcinomatosis or pleural metastases.

questions.question: In which mutation carriers is prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy recommended?
questions.explanation: Prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy is indicated in carriers of BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations to reduce the risk of ovarian and breast cancer.

questions.question: What is the main objective of prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy in BRCA?
questions.explanation: Prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy aims to reduce the risk of ovarian and breast cancer in women with BRCA mutations, it does not treat other conditions.

questions.question: At what age is prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy recommended in BRCA1?
questions.explanation: In BRCA1 carriers, surgery is recommended between 35 and 40 years of age due to the early risk of ovarian cancer; in BRCA2 it can be deferred until 40-45 years.

questions.question: What additional procedure is typically performed with prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy?
questions.explanation: In BRCA carriers, prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy is usually combined with risk-reducing bilateral mastectomy to reduce the incidence of breast cancer.

questions.question: What type of cancer does prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy primarily prevent?
questions.explanation: Prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy reduces the risk of high-grade epithelial ovarian cancer, especially the serous type, associated with BRCA mutations.

questions.question: What is an important adverse effect of prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy?
questions.explanation: Ovarian removal induces early surgical menopause with vasomotor symptoms, osteoporosis and cardiovascular risk, which is why it is considered hormone replacement therapy.
